title: Project Crashing
description: Quiz covering project crashing, schedule compression, critical path, time-cost trade-offs, and practical decision making.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of project crashing?
options: To reduce project scope
To shorten the project's completion time by adding resources
To reduce project quality
To eliminate project risks
questions.explanation: Project crashing shortens the project schedule by allocating additional resources to selected activities.

questions.question: Which project management objective is directly affected by crashing?
options: Project duration
Stakeholder identification
Project scope definition
Procurement planning
questions.explanation: The purpose of crashing is to reduce the total project duration.

questions.question: Which additional resource is commonly used when crashing a project?
options: Additional labor
Fewer team members
Reduced communication
Lower quality standards
questions.explanation: Hiring additional workers or assigning more resources is one of the most common crashing techniques.

questions.question: What is usually the main consequence of crashing a project?
options: Lower project costs
Higher project costs
Reduced project scope
Automatic elimination of project risks
questions.explanation: Crashing normally increases project costs because additional resources, overtime or equipment are required.

questions.question: Which activities should normally be considered for crashing first?
options: Activities with the largest budgets
Activities on the Critical Path
Activities with the longest descriptions
Activities with the highest number of stakeholders
questions.explanation: Only activities on the Critical Path directly affect the project's completion date.

questions.question: Why does crashing usually increase project cost?
options: Because additional resources, overtime or equipment must be provided
Because project scope automatically increases
Because all project risks disappear
Because project quality is reduced
questions.explanation: Reducing activity durations generally requires extra labor, equipment or overtime, increasing project costs.

questions.question: Why would a client request that a project be crashed?
options: To finish earlier and meet an important deadline
To reduce project quality
To eliminate all project costs
To avoid creating a project schedule
questions.explanation: Projects are often crashed to meet contractual deadlines, avoid penalties or respond to market opportunities.

questions.question: What is the relationship between project crashing and the Critical Path?
options: Crashing non-critical activities always shortens the project
Crashing Critical Path activities can reduce the overall project duration
Crashing removes the Critical Path completely
The Critical Path is not considered during crashing
questions.explanation: Only reducing the duration of Critical Path activities normally shortens the overall project schedule.

questions.question: A project manager hires an additional construction crew to complete foundation work faster. This is an example of:
options: Fast Tracking
Project Crashing
Scope Creep
Risk Avoidance
questions.explanation: Adding extra resources to reduce activity duration is the definition of project crashing.

questions.question: A project is already ahead of schedule and has no contractual deadline pressure. What is generally the best decision regarding crashing?
options: Crash the project anyway
Evaluate whether the benefits justify the additional costs before crashing
Crash every activity on the Critical Path
Automatically hire more staff
questions.explanation: Crashing should only be used when the benefits of finishing earlier outweigh the additional costs.

questions.question: A project manager is deciding whether to crash an activity. What should be evaluated first?
options: Whether reducing the activity duration provides enough value to justify the additional cost
Whether the activity has the longest description
Whether the activity uses the most expensive equipment
Whether the project team prefers working overtime
questions.explanation: Crashing should only be used when the benefits of finishing earlier outweigh the additional costs.

questions.question: Which of the following activities would generally NOT benefit from crashing?
options: A Critical Path activity that can be completed faster with additional workers
An activity with significant float that is not on the Critical Path
An activity delayed by a shortage of equipment
An activity that has available overtime opportunities
questions.explanation: Reducing the duration of non-critical activities usually does not reduce the project's overall completion time.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the difference between crashing and fast tracking?
options: Crashing adds resources, while fast tracking overlaps activities that were originally planned sequentially
Crashing reduces project scope, while fast tracking increases project scope
Crashing only applies to software projects, while fast tracking only applies to construction projects
There is no practical difference between the two techniques
questions.explanation: Crashing shortens activities by adding resources, while fast tracking shortens the schedule by performing activities in parallel.

questions.question: What is the primary disadvantage of fast tracking compared to crashing?
options: It usually increases project risk due to overlapping activities
It always requires hiring more employees
It guarantees higher project quality
It eliminates the Critical Path
questions.explanation: Fast tracking increases the likelihood of rework because activities are performed simultaneously instead of sequentially.

questions.question: A software company adds four additional developers to complete a feature earlier. Instead of finishing faster, communication becomes more difficult and progress slows. What principle does this illustrate?
options: Adding more resources does not always reduce activity duration
Critical Path activities cannot be crashed
Fast tracking always reduces risk
Project costs automatically decrease when more people are assigned
questions.explanation: Some activities become less efficient when additional people increase communication and coordination requirements.

questions.question: Why is crashing considered a time-cost trade-off?
options: Reducing project duration usually requires spending additional money
Crashing always reduces both time and cost
Crashing eliminates the need for budgeting
Time and cost are unrelated in project management
questions.explanation: Project managers exchange additional cost for a shorter schedule when they decide to crash a project.

questions.question: A project has two Critical Path activities. Only one of them is crashed. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The overall project duration may not decrease if the other Critical Path remains unchanged
The project will always finish earlier
The Critical Path disappears completely
Project costs automatically decrease
questions.explanation: When multiple critical paths exist, shortening only one path may not reduce the overall project duration.

questions.question: Which project manager is using crashing most effectively?
options: One who crashes every project activity regardless of cost
One who selectively crashes activities that provide the greatest schedule reduction for the lowest additional cost
One who crashes only non-critical activities
One who avoids evaluating project risks before crashing
questions.explanation: Good project managers selectively crash activities that provide the best balance between schedule improvement and additional cost.

questions.question: A client offers a large financial bonus if the project finishes two weeks early. What should the project manager do before deciding to crash the schedule?
options: Evaluate whether the bonus exceeds the additional cost and risk of crashing
Automatically hire as many additional workers as possible
Reduce the project scope immediately
Ignore the client's request because schedules cannot be changed
questions.explanation: Project managers should compare the financial benefit of early completion with the additional costs and risks of schedule compression.

questions.question: Which statement best summarizes good project crashing decisions?
options: Crash every project whenever possible
Only crash when reducing the schedule creates greater value than the additional cost and risk
Never crash because it always increases costs
Crash only after the project has already finished
questions.explanation: Effective project managers use crashing strategically, balancing schedule improvements against increased costs, risks and overall business value.

questions.question: A project manager has identified three activities on the Critical Path. Which activity should generally be crashed first?
options: The activity with the highest additional cost
The activity that provides the greatest reduction in project duration for the lowest additional cost
The activity with the largest project team
The longest activity, regardless of cost
questions.explanation: Effective crashing focuses on achieving the maximum schedule reduction for the minimum additional cost.

questions.question: A project has developed two parallel Critical Paths. Why should the project manager be cautious when crashing only one of them?
options: The project budget will automatically decrease
The unchanged Critical Path may still determine the project's completion date
The second Critical Path disappears automatically
Crashing one path always shortens both paths equally
questions.explanation: When multiple Critical Paths exist, reducing only one may not reduce the project's overall duration because the other path can still control the finish date.

questions.question: A project manager crashes an activity by scheduling extensive overtime. After several weeks, productivity decreases and mistakes increase. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Overtime can eventually reduce productivity due to fatigue
Critical Path activities cannot be crashed
Fast tracking automatically occurred
The project budget became invalid
questions.explanation: While overtime may initially shorten activities, excessive overtime often leads to fatigue, lower productivity and higher error rates.

questions.question: Which situation is the strongest justification for crashing a project?
options: The client offers a financial bonus that exceeds the additional crashing cost
The project manager wants to keep the team busy
The project is progressing exactly as planned
The project has significant schedule float
questions.explanation: Crashing should create greater value than its additional cost. A financial incentive may justify schedule compression.

questions.question: A project manager can either crash the schedule or fast track several activities. Which factor most strongly influences the decision?
options: Whether the organization is willing to accept higher costs, higher risks, or a combination of both
The color of the Gantt chart
The number of project meetings
The age of the project team
questions.explanation: Crashing primarily increases cost, while fast tracking primarily increases risk. The decision depends on which trade-off the organization is willing to accept.

questions.question: A project sponsor requests a completion date three weeks earlier but refuses to increase the budget. What should the project manager do first?
options: Evaluate whether schedule compression is feasible and explain the trade-offs between time, cost, scope and risk
Immediately promise the new completion date
Hire additional workers without approval
Reduce project quality without informing stakeholders
questions.explanation: Project managers should evaluate whether the requested schedule is realistic and communicate the consequences of any schedule compression.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why crashing should not automatically be the first solution when a project falls behind schedule?
options: Crashing may increase costs without solving the underlying cause of the delay
Crashing is prohibited in professional project management
Crashing always reduces project quality
Crashing only works during project initiation
questions.explanation: The project manager should first understand why the delay occurred before deciding whether additional resources are the appropriate solution.

questions.question: A company has a limited budget but faces a strict market launch deadline. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Evaluate both crashing and fast tracking to determine the most appropriate balance between cost, risk and schedule
Automatically crash every Critical Path activity
Ignore the deadline
Double the project budget regardless of business value
questions.explanation: Project managers should compare multiple schedule compression techniques and choose the option that provides the best overall business outcome.

questions.question: Which statement best reflects professional project management when considering project crashing?
options: Every project should be crashed to maximize efficiency
Project crashing is a strategic decision that should only be used when the benefits outweigh the additional costs and risks
Crashing always produces the best project outcome
Crashing eliminates the need for project monitoring
questions.explanation: Crashing is a management decision based on evaluating business value, costs, risks and project objectives rather than simply shortening the schedule.

questions.question: What is the most important lesson about project crashing?
options: The shortest possible schedule is always the best schedule
Successful project managers balance time, cost, scope, quality and risk before deciding to compress a project schedule
Additional resources always improve productivity
Only construction projects use project crashing
questions.explanation: Project crashing is ultimately about making informed trade-offs. Effective project managers consider all project constraints—not just time—before deciding to shorten a schedule.
